cards.title: Classic Clinical Signs — Cardiovascular & Respiratory
cards._legacyHtml:
SignDescriptionConditionSensitivity
Corrigan's pulseBounding, collapsing water-hammer pulseAortic regurgitationModerate
Duroziez's signSystolic + diastolic murmur over femoral arteryAortic regurgitationHigh specificity
Quincke's signVisible nail bed capillary pulsationAortic regurgitationLow
Ewart's signDullness + bronchial breathing below left scapulaPericardial effusionModerate
Kussmaul's signJVP rises on inspiration (paradox)Constrictive pericarditis, RHFHigh for constrictive
Trousseau's signCarpal spasm on BP cuff inflation (3 min)Hypocalcaemia94%
Chvostek's signFacial twitch on tapping CN VIIHypocalcaemia29% (low — many false positives)
Pemberton's signFacial congestion + arm raise (SVC obstruction)Retrosternal goitre, mediastinal massHigh specificity
Hamman's signCrunching sound synchronous with heartbeatPneumomediastinumHigh specificity
Tracheal tug (Oliver's)Downward tracheal movement on inspirationAortic aneurysmLow sensitivity
🟡 Pearl — Trousseau vs Chvostek
Trousseau's sign (95% sensitive) is FAR more reliable than Chvostek's (29% sensitive, 70% false positive rate — present in 10-25% of normal people). If you suspect hypocalcaemia, rely on Trousseau's: inflate BP cuff 20mmHg above systolic for 3 minutes — carpopedal spasm (main en accoucheur position) = positive.

cards.title: Classic Clinical Signs — GI & Abdominal
cards._legacyHtml:
SignDescriptionCondition
Murphy's sign ★Inspiratory arrest on deep palpation RUQAcute cholecystitis (97% sensitive)
McBurney's signTenderness at 1/3 from ASIS to umbilicusAcute appendicitis
Rovsing's signRIF pain on LIF palpationAcute appendicitis
Psoas signRIF pain on right hip extensionRetrocaecal appendicitis
Obturator signRIF pain on internal hip rotation (flexed)Pelvic appendicitis
Cullen's signPeriumbilical bruisingHaemoperitoneum, severe pancreatitis
Grey Turner's signFlank bruisingRetroperitoneal haemorrhage, pancreatitis
Courvoisier's signPalpable non-tender gallbladder + jaundicePancreatic head cancer (NOT stones)
Sister Mary Joseph's noduleUmbilical noduleIntra-abdominal malignancy — poor prognosis
Shifting dullnessDullness shifts with position changeAscites (>1500ml)
Fluid thrillTransmitted impulse through ascitesTense ascites (>2000ml)

cards.title: Neurological & MSK Clinical Signs
cards._legacyHtml:
SignTestPositive FindingCondition
Babinski's sign ★Stroke lateral sole of footUpgoing hallux (extensor plantar)UMN lesion (normal in infants <2yr)
Romberg's signStand feet together, eyes closedSwaying/falling with eyes closed onlyPosterior column lesion, sensory ataxia
Kernig's signHip flexed 90°, extend kneeResistance/pain at <135° extensionMeningeal irritation, meningitis
Brudzinski's signPassive neck flexionInvoluntary hip/knee flexionMeningeal irritation
Lhermitte's signNeck flexionElectric shock down spineMultiple sclerosis, cervical myelopathy
Lasègue's sign (SLR)Straight leg raise 30–70°Radicular pain below kneeL4/L5/S1 disc herniation (80% sensitive)
Spurling's testNeck extension + lateral bend + axial loadIpsilateral arm radiculopathyCervical nerve root compression
Phalen's signWrist flexion held 60 secParaesthesia in median nerve distributionCarpal tunnel syndrome (75% sensitive)
Tinel's signPercussion over carpal tunnelTingling in median nerve distributionCarpal tunnel (67% sensitive)
Finkelstein's testThumb tucked, ulnar deviation wristSevere radial wrist painDe Quervain's tenosynovitis
🟡 UMN vs LMN Signs
UMN (above spinal cord): spasticity, hyperreflexia, upgoing plantar, clonus, no wasting. LMN (at or below cord): flaccidity, hyporeflexia, downgoing plantar, fasciculations, wasting. Babinski's positive = UMN lesion ANYWHERE above L1. In isolation after stroke: ipsilateral weakness + contralateral Babinski = crossed signs = brainstem lesion.

cards.title: Naturopathic Diagnostic Signs — Tongue, Nail & Eye
cards._legacyHtml:
FindingLocationNaturopathic SignificanceConsider
Pale tongueWhole tongueBlood deficiency, anaemia, coldIron, B12, folate deficiency
Red tongue, no coatWhole tongueYin deficiency, heat excessDehydration, fever, hyperthyroidism
Geographic tonguePatchy coating lossDigestive weakness, stressNutritional deficiency, IBS
Thick white coatPosterior tongueDampness, digestive accumulationCandida, SIBO, low HCl
Yellow coatPosterior tongueHeat, liver/gallbladder stressHepatobiliary issue, infection
Cracks/fissuresCentre of tongueStomach Yin deficiencyChronic gastritis, stress
KoilonychiaSpoon-shaped nailsIron deficiency anaemiaFerritin, CBC
LeukonychiaWhite spots/bandsZinc deficiency (popular belief — evidence weak)Zinc, minor trauma
Beau's linesTransverse ridgesSystemic illness, severe stress, malnutritionTimeline: 1mm = 1 month of growth
ClubbingNail angle >180°Chronic hypoxia, liver diseaseCXR, LFTs, SpO2 — refer
Arcus senilisGrey ring around irisLipid deposition — cardiovascular riskFasting lipid profile if <50 years
XanthelasmaYellow plaques eyelidsHypercholesterolaemiaLipid profile — 50% have dyslipidaemia
